water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: Light and pressure do not strongly push the outlook either way.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing conditions on the Chattahoochee River are very good with ideal water temperatures and recent stocking enhancing the outlook. Expect active feeding with a balanced nymph and stocked trout attractor approach.
generated_text.best_window: Day 1 morning is the best window due to favorable water temperature, moderate sky cover, and optimal trout positioning in riffles, runs, seams, and structure enabling effective use of nymphs, midges, and soft hackles.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 57.9°F, flow is 933 cfs, gage height is 2.01 ft, and turbidity is 6.0 NTU, indicating clear and ideal tailwater conditions for trout.
generated_text.weather_setup: Skies are partly cloudy with 21% average cover, pressure at 30.05 inHg with stable trend, light southwest winds averaging 5.3 mph, and only a 13% chance of rain expected, providing stable fishing weather.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will favor comfortable feeding lanes near cover such as riffles and seams. Use clean drifts with smaller natural patterns and stocked-trout attractors like egg patterns, squirmy worms, and duracell. Focus on nymphs, midges, and soft hackles while remaining adaptable to opportunistic streamer presentations.
